chunks.title: Passive Voice - Definition
chunks.question: What is the passive voice rule?
chunks.answer: A sentence uses passive voice when the subject receives the action instead of performing it. Pattern: 'subject + be verb + past participle (+ by actor)'

chunks.title: Passive Voice - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the passive voice rule matter?
chunks.answer: Passive voice obscures responsibility and makes writing less direct. Active voice clarifies who does what, improving clarity and readability.

chunks.title: Passive Voice - Detection
chunks.question: How is the passive voice rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using spaCy dependency parsing looking for 'auxpass' dependencies, indicating passive auxiliary verbs like 'is done', 'was created', 'has been reviewed'.

chunks.title: Passive Voice - Exception
chunks.question: When should the passive voice rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Passive voice is acceptable when: 1) The actor is unknown or irrelevant ('The building was constructed in 1920'), 2) In scientific writing where the process matters more than the actor, 3) When the object is more important than the subject, 4) In titles and headings.

chunks.title: PASSIVE_VOICE - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating PASSIVE_VOICE be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The document was reviewed by the team.

After: The team reviewed the document.

Why: Active voice makes the sentence more direct and identifies the actor clearly.

chunks.title: Passive Voice - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the passive voice rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "The system was designed to handle multiple users."

Reason: When the designer is unknown or irrelevant, passive voice is acceptable.

chunks.title: Long Sentences - Definition
chunks.question: What is the long sentences rule?
chunks.answer: Sentences exceeding 25 words are flagged as potentially difficult to read. Long sentences often contain multiple ideas that should be split.

chunks.title: Long Sentences - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the long sentences rule matter?
chunks.answer: Long sentences reduce readability and comprehension. Readers must hold too much information in working memory. Breaking long sentences improves clarity and maintains reader engagement.

chunks.title: Long Sentences - Detection
chunks.question: How is the long sentences rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using spaCy sentence segmentation. Word count is calculated per sentence. Titles, headings, and markdown table separators are excluded.

chunks.title: Long Sentences - Exception
chunks.question: When should the long sentences rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Long sentences acceptable when: 1) In titles or headings, 2) In markdown table rows, 3) When listing related items that form a coherent unit, 4) In legal or technical definitions where precision requires length.

chunks.title: LONG_SENTENCES - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating LONG_SENTENCES be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The system processes incoming requests by first validating the user credentials, then checking the request parameters against the schema, and finally forwarding the validated request to the appropriate handler for processing.

After: The system processes incoming requests in three steps. First, it validates user credentials. Next, it checks request parameters against the schema. Finally, it forwards the validated request to the appropriate handler.

Why: Breaking the long sentence into clear steps improves readability and makes the process easier to follow.

chunks.title: Long Sentences - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the long sentences rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "Configure the database connection string in the settings file."

Reason: This sentence is under 25 words and expresses a single, clear action.

chunks.title: Weak Adverbs - Definition
chunks.question: What is the weak adverbs rule?
chunks.answer: Adverbs ending in '-ly' often weaken writing by modifying verbs that could be stronger. Examples: very quickly, really important, extremely difficult.

chunks.title: Weak Adverbs - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the weak adverbs rule matter?
chunks.answer: Adverbs can make writing imprecise and wordy. Choosing stronger, more specific verbs eliminates the need for adverbs and makes writing more powerful.

chunks.title: Weak Adverbs - Detection
chunks.question: How is the weak adverbs rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using spaCy POS tagging. Tokens tagged as 'ADV' and ending with 'ly' are flagged. Titles and headings are excluded.

chunks.title: Weak Adverbs - Exception
chunks.question: When should the weak adverbs rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Adverbs acceptable when: 1) In titles or headings, 2) When precision requires degree specification ('approximately', 'roughly'), 3) In technical contexts where the adverb has specific meaning ('asynchronously', 'recursively'), 4) In quotations or UI labels.

chunks.title: ADVERBS - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating ADVERBS be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The application runs very quickly on modern hardware.

After: The application runs fast on modern hardware.

Why: 'Very' is redundant - 'fast' is sufficient and more direct.

chunks.title: Weak Adverbs - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the weak adverbs rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "The function executes asynchronously."

Reason: 'Asynchronously' is a technical term with specific meaning that cannot be replaced.

chunks.title: Overuse of 'Very' - Definition
chunks.question: What is the overuse of 'very' rule?
chunks.answer: 'Very' is often an intensifier that adds no real meaning. It typically indicates the writer should choose a more precise word.

chunks.title: Overuse of 'Very' - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the overuse of 'very' rule matter?
chunks.answer: 'Very' weakens writing by padding sentences without adding value. Removing 'very' or choosing stronger words makes writing more impactful.

chunks.title: Overuse of 'Very' - Detection
chunks.question: How is the overuse of 'very' rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using case-insensitive regex matching for the word 'very'. Excludes titles and headings.

chunks.title: Overuse of 'Very' - Exception
chunks.question: When should the overuse of 'very' rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: 'Very' acceptable in: 1) Titles or headings, 2) Direct quotations, 3) UI labels or button text, 4) When part of a fixed expression ('very well', 'very best').

chunks.title: VERY - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating VERY be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: This is a very important decision.

After: This is a critical decision.

Why: 'Critical' is more precise and impactful than 'very important'.

chunks.title: Overuse of 'Very' - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the overuse of 'very' rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "Thank you very much."

Reason: Fixed expression where 'very much' is idiomatic and appropriate.

chunks.title: Basic Grammar - Definition
chunks.question: What is the basic grammar rule?
chunks.answer: Common grammar issues including: double spaces, lack of punctuation in complex sentences, and basic sentence structure problems.

chunks.title: Basic Grammar - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the basic grammar rule matter?
chunks.answer: Grammar errors reduce professionalism and clarity. They distract readers from content and reduce credibility.

chunks.title: Basic Grammar - Detection
chunks.question: How is the basic grammar rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using regex patterns for double spaces and spaCy for sentence structure analysis.

chunks.title: Basic Grammar - Exception
chunks.question: When should the basic grammar rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Some patterns acceptable in: 1) Code blocks or technical specifications, 2) Intentional formatting (like indentation), 3) UI mock-ups or wireframes.

chunks.title: GRAMMAR_BASIC - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating GRAMMAR_BASIC be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The  system handles  multiple requests.

After: The system handles multiple requests.

Why: Removed double spaces for proper formatting.

chunks.title: Basic Grammar - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the basic grammar rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "Code example with intentional  spacing."

Reason: Code blocks may require specific spacing for alignment.

chunks.title: Terminology Consistency - Definition
chunks.question: What is the terminology consistency rule?
chunks.answer: Using consistent terms throughout documentation prevents confusion. Synonyms should be avoided when referring to the same concept.

chunks.title: Terminology Consistency - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the terminology consistency rule matter?
chunks.answer: Inconsistent terminology confuses readers and suggests imprecision. Technical documentation especially requires exact, consistent term usage.

chunks.title: Terminology Consistency - Detection
chunks.question: How is the terminology consistency rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected through term tracking and pattern matching. Looks for multiple terms referring to the same concept within a document.

chunks.title: Terminology Consistency - Exception
chunks.question: When should the terminology consistency rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Variation acceptable when: 1) Introducing a term ('user interface (UI)'), 2) Literary variation in non-technical content, 3) When terms have genuinely different meanings in context.

chunks.title: CONSISTENCY - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating CONSISTENCY be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: Click the button. Press the control to continue.

After: Click the button. Click the button to continue.

Why: Consistent terminology ('button') prevents confusion about whether 'control' is different from 'button'.

chunks.title: Terminology Consistency - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the terminology consistency rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "The user interface (UI) displays information."

Reason: Introducing an abbreviation requires showing both forms.

chunks.title: Readability - Definition
chunks.question: What is the readability rule?
chunks.answer: Complex words and jargon should be replaced with simpler alternatives when possible, without sacrificing precision.

chunks.title: Readability - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the readability rule matter?
chunks.answer: Simpler language reaches a broader audience and improves comprehension. Unnecessary complexity alienates readers.

chunks.title: Readability - Detection
chunks.question: How is the readability rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using syllable counting and word frequency analysis. Flags words with 3+ syllables that have simpler alternatives.

chunks.title: Readability - Exception
chunks.question: When should the readability rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Complex words acceptable when: 1) Technical terms with no simpler equivalent, 2) Industry-standard terminology, 3) Legal or regulatory language, 4) Proper nouns or titles.

chunks.title: READABILITY - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating READABILITY be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: Utilize the functionality to accomplish the task.

After: Use the feature to complete the task.

Why: 'Use', 'feature', and 'complete' are simpler and equally precise.

chunks.title: Readability - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the readability rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "Configure the authentication parameters."

Reason: 'Authentication' is a standard technical term without a simpler alternative.

chunks.title: Nominalizations - Definition
chunks.question: What is the nominalizations rule?
chunks.answer: Nominalizations are verbs turned into nouns (utilization → utilize, implementation → implement). They make writing wordier and less direct.

chunks.title: Nominalizations - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the nominalizations rule matter?
chunks.answer: Nominalizations add unnecessary words and obscure action. Converting them back to verbs creates clearer, more direct sentences.

chunks.title: Nominalizations - Detection
chunks.question: How is the nominalizations rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using pattern matching for common nominalization suffixes (-tion, -ment, -ance, -ence) and checking if a verb form exists.

chunks.title: Nominalizations - Exception
chunks.question: When should the nominalizations rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Nominalizations acceptable when: 1) They're established technical terms ('implementation plan'), 2) When used as subjects in sentences about concepts, 3) In titles or headings.

chunks.title: NOMINALIZATIONS - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating NOMINALIZATIONS be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The implementation of the feature requires validation.

After: Implementing the feature requires validation.

Why: Using the verb 'implementing' is more direct than the noun 'implementation'.

chunks.title: Nominalizations - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the nominalizations rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "The implementation plan outlines the approach."

Reason: 'Implementation plan' is an established term referring to a specific document type.

chunks.title: Vague Terms - Definition
chunks.question: What is the vague terms rule?
chunks.answer: Vague terms like 'things', 'stuff', 'various', 'several' lack precision and should be replaced with specific details.

chunks.title: Vague Terms - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the vague terms rule matter?
chunks.answer: Vague terms force readers to guess meaning. Specific language eliminates ambiguity and improves clarity.

chunks.title: Vague Terms - Detection
chunks.question: How is the vague terms rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using pattern matching for common vague terms. Context analysis determines if specificity is missing.

chunks.title: Vague Terms - Exception
chunks.question: When should the vague terms rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Vague terms acceptable when: 1) Specificity is genuinely not available, 2) In casual communication, 3) When listing examples where exhaustive detail is unnecessary.

chunks.title: VAGUE_TERMS - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating VAGUE_TERMS be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The system handles various types of requests.

After: The system handles GET, POST, and DELETE requests.

Why: Specific request types are more informative than 'various types'.

chunks.title: Vague Terms - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the vague terms rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "Results may vary based on configuration."

Reason: When outcomes genuinely depend on many factors, 'vary' is acceptable.

chunks.title: Verb Tense Consistency - Definition
chunks.question: What is the verb tense consistency rule?
chunks.answer: Inconsistent verb tenses within a paragraph or section confuse readers about timing and sequence.

chunks.title: Verb Tense Consistency - Importance
chunks.question: Why does the verb tense consistency rule matter?
chunks.answer: Tense shifts make it unclear when actions occur. Consistent tense maintains clear temporal relationships.

chunks.title: Verb Tense Consistency - Detection
chunks.question: How is the verb tense consistency rule detected?
chunks.answer: Detected using spaCy POS tagging to track verb tenses within sentences and paragraphs.

chunks.title: Verb Tense Consistency - Exception
chunks.question: When should the verb tense consistency rule NOT be applied?
chunks.answer: Tense changes acceptable when: 1) Describing events at genuinely different times, 2) In conditional statements, 3) When quoting or referencing other sources.

chunks.title: VERB_TENSE - Rewrite Example
chunks.question: How should text violating VERB_TENSE be rewritten?
chunks.answer:
Before: The user clicks the button and the system displayed the result.

After: The user clicks the button and the system displays the result.

Why: Consistent present tense maintains clear action sequence.

chunks.title: Verb Tense Consistency - When NOT to Rewrite
chunks.question: When should the verb tense consistency rule NOT trigger a rewrite?
chunks.answer:
Example: "The system now displays results, but previously it showed errors."

Reason: Tense change appropriately indicates different time periods.

chunks.title: Bad Example: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The document was reviewed by the team.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the PASSIVE_VOICE rule. Converted from passive to active voice to identify the actor and make the sentence more direct.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The document was reviewed by the team.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The team reviewed the document.'. Converted from passive to active voice to identify the actor and make the sentence more direct.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: Why was 'The document was reviewed by the team.' rewritten to 'The team reviewed the document.'?
chunks.answer: Converted from passive to active voice to identify the actor and make the sentence more direct.

chunks.title: Bad Example: LONG_SENTENCES
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The system processes incoming requests by first validating the user credentials, then checking the request parameters against the schema, and finally forwarding the validated request to the appropriate handler for processing.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the LONG_SENTENCES rule. Broke long 42-word sentence into 4 shorter sentences to improve readability and comprehension.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: LONG_SENTENCES
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The system processes incoming requests by first validating the user credentials, then checking the request parameters against the schema, and finally forwarding the validated request to the appropriate handler for processing.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The system processes incoming requests in three steps. First, it validates user credentials. Next, it checks request parameters against the schema. Finally, it forwards the validated request to the appropriate handler.'. Broke long 42-word sentence into 4 shorter sentences to improve readability and comprehension.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: LONG_SENTENCES
chunks.question: Why was 'The system processes incoming requests by first validating the user credentials, then checking the request parameters against the schema, and finally forwarding the validated request to the appropriate handler for processing.' rewritten to 'The system processes incoming requests in three steps. First, it validates user credentials. Next, it checks request parameters against the schema. Finally, it forwards the validated request to the appropriate handler.'?
chunks.answer: Broke long 42-word sentence into 4 shorter sentences to improve readability and comprehension.

chunks.title: Bad Example: ADVERBS
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The application runs very quickly on modern hardware.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the ADVERBS rule. Removed unnecessary 'very' - the word 'quickly' already conveys speed, and 'fast' is more direct.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: ADVERBS
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The application runs very quickly on modern hardware.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The application runs fast on modern hardware.'. Removed unnecessary 'very' - the word 'quickly' already conveys speed, and 'fast' is more direct.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: ADVERBS
chunks.question: Why was 'The application runs very quickly on modern hardware.' rewritten to 'The application runs fast on modern hardware.'?
chunks.answer: Removed unnecessary 'very' - the word 'quickly' already conveys speed, and 'fast' is more direct.

chunks.title: Bad Example: NOMINALIZATIONS
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The implementation of the new feature requires validation of user input.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the NOMINALIZATIONS rule. Converted nominalizations 'implementation' and 'validation' to verb forms for more direct writing.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: NOMINALIZATIONS
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The implementation of the new feature requires validation of user input.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'Implementing the new feature requires validating user input.'. Converted nominalizations 'implementation' and 'validation' to verb forms for more direct writing.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: NOMINALIZATIONS
chunks.question: Why was 'The implementation of the new feature requires validation of user input.' rewritten to 'Implementing the new feature requires validating user input.'?
chunks.answer: Converted nominalizations 'implementation' and 'validation' to verb forms for more direct writing.

chunks.title: Bad Example: VAGUE_TERMS
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The system handles various types of requests.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the VAGUE_TERMS rule. Replaced vague 'various types' with specific request types for clarity.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: VAGUE_TERMS
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The system handles various types of requests.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The system handles GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE requests.'. Replaced vague 'various types' with specific request types for clarity.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: VAGUE_TERMS
chunks.question: Why was 'The system handles various types of requests.' rewritten to 'The system handles GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE requests.'?
chunks.answer: Replaced vague 'various types' with specific request types for clarity.

chunks.title: Bad Example: VERY
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'This is a very important security consideration.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the VERY rule. Replaced 'very important' with more precise word 'critical'.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: VERY
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'This is a very important security consideration.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'This is a critical security consideration.'. Replaced 'very important' with more precise word 'critical'.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: VERY
chunks.question: Why was 'This is a very important security consideration.' rewritten to 'This is a critical security consideration.'?
chunks.answer: Replaced 'very important' with more precise word 'critical'.

chunks.title: Bad Example: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'Errors are logged by the monitoring system.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the PASSIVE_VOICE rule. Active voice clearly identifies what performs the action.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'Errors are logged by the monitoring system.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The monitoring system logs errors.'. Active voice clearly identifies what performs the action.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: PASSIVE_VOICE
chunks.question: Why was 'Errors are logged by the monitoring system.' rewritten to 'The monitoring system logs errors.'?
chunks.answer: Active voice clearly identifies what performs the action.

chunks.title: Bad Example: READABILITY
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'Utilize the functionality to accomplish the task.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the READABILITY rule. Replaced complex words with simpler alternatives: 'utilize'→'use', 'functionality'→'feature', 'accomplish'→'complete'.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: READABILITY
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'Utilize the functionality to accomplish the task.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'Use the feature to complete the task.'. Replaced complex words with simpler alternatives: 'utilize'→'use', 'functionality'→'feature', 'accomplish'→'complete'.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: READABILITY
chunks.question: Why was 'Utilize the functionality to accomplish the task.' rewritten to 'Use the feature to complete the task.'?
chunks.answer: Replaced complex words with simpler alternatives: 'utilize'→'use', 'functionality'→'feature', 'accomplish'→'complete'.

chunks.title: Bad Example: CONSISTENCY
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'Click the button. Press the control to continue. Hit the switch to finalize.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the CONSISTENCY rule. Used consistent terminology 'button' throughout instead of mixing 'button', 'control', 'switch'.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: CONSISTENCY
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'Click the button. Press the control to continue. Hit the switch to finalize.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'Click the button. Click the button to continue. Click the button to finalize.'. Used consistent terminology 'button' throughout instead of mixing 'button', 'control', 'switch'.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: CONSISTENCY
chunks.question: Why was 'Click the button. Press the control to continue. Hit the switch to finalize.' rewritten to 'Click the button. Click the button to continue. Click the button to finalize.'?
chunks.answer: Used consistent terminology 'button' throughout instead of mixing 'button', 'control', 'switch'.

chunks.title: Bad Example: VERB_TENSE
chunks.question: What is wrong with this sentence: 'The user clicks the button and the system displayed the result.'?
chunks.answer: This sentence violates the VERB_TENSE rule. Maintained consistent present tense throughout the sentence.

chunks.title: Corrected Example: VERB_TENSE
chunks.question: How should this be written correctly: 'The user clicks the button and the system displayed the result.'?
chunks.answer: Correct version: 'The user clicks the button and the system displays the result.'. Maintained consistent present tense throughout the sentence.

chunks.title: Why Rewrite: VERB_TENSE
chunks.question: Why was 'The user clicks the button and the system displayed the result.' rewritten to 'The user clicks the button and the system displays the result.'?
chunks.answer: Maintained consistent present tense throughout the sentence.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels
chunks.question: Should UI button labels be rewritten?
chunks.answer: NO. UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 1
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'Save'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 2
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'Cancel'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 3
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'Submit'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 4
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'Delete'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 5
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'OK'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: UI button labels - Example 6
chunks.question: Should this UI button labels be rewritten: 'Apply'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite UI button labels. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: UI labels must match the actual interface exactly. Rewriting them will confuse users who cannot find the button you describe.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: menu items
chunks.question: Should menu items be rewritten?
chunks.answer: NO. Menu items must match the product interface exactly. Users follow documentation to find specific menus.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: menu items - Example 1
chunks.question: Should this menu items be rewritten: 'File > Open'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite menu items. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Menu items must match the product interface exactly. Users follow documentation to find specific menus.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: menu items - Example 2
chunks.question: Should this menu items be rewritten: 'Edit > Preferences'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite menu items. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Menu items must match the product interface exactly. Users follow documentation to find specific menus.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: menu items - Example 3
chunks.question: Should this menu items be rewritten: 'View > Toolbar'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite menu items. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Menu items must match the product interface exactly. Users follow documentation to find specific menus.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: field labels in forms
chunks.question: Should field labels in forms be rewritten?
chunks.answer: NO. Field labels must match the UI exactly so users can locate the correct input field.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: field labels in forms - Example 1
chunks.question: Should this field labels in forms be rewritten: 'Username'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite field labels in forms. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Field labels must match the UI exactly so users can locate the correct input field.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: field labels in forms - Example 2
chunks.question: Should this field labels in forms be rewritten: 'Email Address'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite field labels in forms. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Field labels must match the UI exactly so users can locate the correct input field.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: field labels in forms - Example 3
chunks.question: Should this field labels in forms be rewritten: 'Password'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite field labels in forms. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Field labels must match the UI exactly so users can locate the correct input field.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: field labels in forms - Example 4
chunks.question: Should this field labels in forms be rewritten: 'Confirm Password'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite field labels in forms. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Field labels must match the UI exactly so users can locate the correct input field.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: dialog box titles
chunks.question: Should dialog box titles be rewritten?
chunks.answer: NO. Dialog titles must match exactly so users can identify which window the documentation refers to.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: dialog box titles - Example 1
chunks.question: Should this dialog box titles be rewritten: 'Save As'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite dialog box titles. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Dialog titles must match exactly so users can identify which window the documentation refers to.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: dialog box titles - Example 2
chunks.question: Should this dialog box titles be rewritten: 'Preferences'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite dialog box titles. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Dialog titles must match exactly so users can identify which window the documentation refers to.

chunks.title: Do NOT rewrite: dialog box titles - Example 3
chunks.question: Should this dialog box titles be rewritten: 'Error'?
chunks.answer: NO. Never rewrite dialog box titles. They must remain exactly as shown. Reason: Dialog titles must match exactly so users can identify which window the documentation refers to.

chunks.title: Procedure Structure - Rule 1
chunks.question: How should a procedure be structured?
chunks.answer: A procedure should have: 1) Clear title stating the goal, 2) Prerequisites or preconditions, 3) Numbered steps in sequential order, 4) Expected result or outcome.

chunks.title: Procedure Structure - Rule 2
chunks.question: When should procedure steps be numbered?
chunks.answer: Use numbered steps when order matters. Steps must be performed in sequence. If steps can be done in any order, use bullets instead.

chunks.title: Procedure Structure - Rule 3
chunks.question: How long should a procedure step be?
chunks.answer: Each step should be one action. If a step requires multiple actions, break it into substeps. Maximum one sentence per step.

chunks.title: Procedure Structure - Rule 4
chunks.question: Should procedures include explanations?
chunks.answer: Procedures should focus on actions. Keep explanations brief. If extensive explanation needed, add a separate 'About' section before the procedure.

chunks.title: Procedure Structure - Rule 5
chunks.question: How should procedure preconditions be presented?
chunks.answer: List preconditions before step 1 using a 'Before you begin' or 'Prerequisites' section. Use bullets for multiple preconditions.

chunks.title: List Formatting - Rule 1
chunks.question: When should I use bulleted lists?
chunks.answer: Use bullets for items without sequence or priority. All items are equal in importance and can be read in any order.

chunks.title: List Formatting - Rule 2
chunks.question: When should I use numbered lists?
chunks.answer: Use numbers for: 1) Sequential steps, 2) Ranked items (first, second, third), 3) Items that will be referenced by number.

chunks.title: List Formatting - Rule 3
chunks.question: How should list items be capitalized?
chunks.answer: If list items are complete sentences, capitalize first word and use end punctuation. If fragments, use lowercase (unless proper noun) and no punctuation.

chunks.title: List Formatting - Rule 4
chunks.question: Should list items be parallel in structure?
chunks.answer: Yes. All items in a list must use parallel grammatical structure. If first item starts with a verb, all items must start with verbs.

chunks.title: List Formatting - Rule 5
chunks.question: How many items should a list have?
chunks.answer: Minimum 2 items for a list. If more than 7-9 items, consider grouping into categories or splitting into multiple lists.

chunks.title: Table Structure - Rule 1
chunks.question: When should information be presented in a table?
chunks.answer: Use tables for: 1) Comparing multiple items across same attributes, 2) Reference data, 3) Specifications, 4) Configurations. Avoid tables for simple lists.

chunks.title: Table Structure - Rule 2
chunks.question: How should table headers be formatted?
chunks.answer: Table headers should be concise (1-3 words), use title case, and clearly identify column content. First column typically identifies rows.

chunks.title: Table Structure - Rule 3
chunks.question: Should tables have descriptions?
chunks.answer: Yes. Introduce every table with a sentence explaining its purpose. Use 'The following table...' or 'Table X shows...'.

chunks.title: Table Structure - Rule 4
chunks.question: How should table cells be formatted?
chunks.answer: Keep cell content concise. Use sentence fragments, not full sentences. Align text left, numbers right. Use consistent formatting within columns.

chunks.title: Table Structure - Rule 5
chunks.question: When should a table be split?
chunks.answer: Split tables that: 1) Don't fit on one page/screen, 2) Have more than 7-8 columns, 3) Mix different types of information.

chunks.title: Heading Hierarchy - Rule 1
chunks.question: How should heading levels be used?
chunks.answer: Use heading hierarchy to show document structure. H1 for main title, H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections. Never skip levels (H1 to H3).

chunks.title: Heading Hierarchy - Rule 2
chunks.question: How should headings be capitalized?
chunks.answer: Use sentence case (capitalize first word only) or title case (capitalize major words). Be consistent throughout the document. Sentence case is more modern and readable.

chunks.title: Heading Hierarchy - Rule 3
chunks.question: Should headings be numbered?
chunks.answer: Number headings when: 1) Document will be referenced by section number, 2) Formal technical or legal document, 3) Multi-level TOC needed. Otherwise, use unnumbered.

chunks.title: Heading Hierarchy - Rule 4
chunks.question: How long should a heading be?
chunks.answer: Headings should be concise: 2-8 words. Use nouns and verbs, avoid articles (a, an, the). Heading should clearly identify section content.

chunks.title: Heading Hierarchy - Rule 5
chunks.question: Should headings be complete sentences?
chunks.answer: No. Headings should be phrases, not sentences. No end punctuation (unless question). Exception: FAQ sections can use questions as headings.

chunks.title: Notes and Warnings - Rule 1
chunks.question: When should I use a Note?
chunks.answer: Use NOTE for helpful supplementary information that enhances understanding but isn't critical. Reader can skip without losing comprehension.

chunks.title: Notes and Warnings - Rule 2
chunks.question: When should I use a Warning?
chunks.answer: Use WARNING for actions that could cause data loss, system damage, or safety issues. Warnings are critical and must not be ignored.

chunks.title: Notes and Warnings - Rule 3
chunks.question: When should I use a Caution?
chunks.answer: Use CAUTION for actions that could cause unexpected results or minor problems. Less severe than warning but more important than note.

chunks.title: Notes and Warnings - Rule 4
chunks.question: When should I use a Tip?
chunks.answer: Use TIP for best practices, shortcuts, or expert advice that improves efficiency or quality. Tips add value but aren't required knowledge.

chunks.title: Notes and Warnings - Rule 5
chunks.question: How should admonitions be formatted?
chunks.answer: Place admonitions before the step or paragraph they relate to. Keep text concise (1-3 sentences). Use specific, actionable language.

chunks.title: Code Examples - Rule 1
chunks.question: How should code examples be introduced?
chunks.answer: Introduce code with a sentence explaining what it does and when to use it. Example: 'The following code authenticates the user:'

chunks.title: Code Examples - Rule 2
chunks.question: Should code examples be complete or excerpts?
chunks.answer: Provide complete, runnable examples when possible. If showing excerpt, indicate with comments where omitted code goes: '// ... rest of function'

chunks.title: Code Examples - Rule 3
chunks.question: Should code examples include comments?
chunks.answer: Yes. Comment non-obvious code. Explain why, not what (code shows what). Focus comments on business logic and key decisions.

chunks.title: Code Examples - Rule 4
chunks.question: How should code examples be tested?
chunks.answer: All code examples must be tested and verified to work. Broken examples destroy user trust and waste debugging time.

chunks.title: Code Examples - Rule 5
chunks.question: Should inline code use different formatting than code blocks?
chunks.answer: Yes. Use inline code for: single keywords, short expressions, function names. Use code blocks for: multiple lines, complete functions, runnable examples.

chunks.title: Cross-references - Rule 1
chunks.question: How should I write cross-references?
chunks.answer: Use descriptive link text that works out of context. Avoid 'click here' or 'this link'. Example: 'See the Installation Guide' not 'See this page'.

chunks.title: Cross-references - Rule 2
chunks.question: When should I link to other sections?
chunks.answer: Link when: 1) Referenced topic explained elsewhere, 2) User needs prerequisite knowledge, 3) Avoiding duplication. Don't link for common knowledge.

chunks.title: Cross-references - Rule 3
chunks.question: Should external links be marked differently?
chunks.answer: Yes. Indicate external links (to other sites) with icon or text. Users need to know when they're leaving your documentation.

chunks.title: Cross-references - Rule 4
chunks.question: How should I reference figures and tables?
chunks.answer: Reference by number and type: 'Figure 1 shows...', 'See Table 2'. Place reference before the figure/table, never after.

chunks.title: Error Message Documentation - Rule 1
chunks.question: How should error messages be documented?
chunks.answer: Document errors with: 1) Exact error text (in code format), 2) What causes the error, 3) How to fix it, 4) How to prevent it.

chunks.title: Error Message Documentation - Rule 2
chunks.question: Should error documentation include error codes?
chunks.answer: Yes. Include exact error code if system generates one. Users search for error codes when troubleshooting.

chunks.title: Error Message Documentation - Rule 3
chunks.question: How should troubleshooting steps be presented?
chunks.answer: Present troubleshooting as: 1) Check this first (most common cause), 2) Check this next, 3) Last resort steps. Order by likelihood.
